title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway for Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa holds stories of ancestral journeys, resource gathering, and environmental stewardship. Students will connect with local kaumātua to understand the traditional narratives and contemporary challenges facing the awa, using these insights to create contemporary carvings that speak to both heritage and future guardianship.
learningObjectives: Research and document at least three traditional pūrākau (stories) associated with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying key symbols and themes relevant to whakairo.
Design and create a small-scale whakairo (wood carving) that incorporates traditional motifs from Ngāti Kahungunu, reflecting a story or value connected to the awa.
Present their whakairo to peers and a local kaumātua, explaining the cultural significance of their design choices and its connection to community and environmental stewardship.
Collaborate in a group to create a collective art piece that addresses a contemporary issue facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, such as pollution or water conservation.

activities.title: He Hīkoi Awa: Journey Along the Stream
activities.description: Students will take a guided hīkoi (walk) along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua or knowledge holder. They will observe the natural environment, listen to pūrākau (stories) of the awa, and sketch natural forms, patterns, and potential symbols for their carvings. Focus on understanding the mauri (life force) of the waterway.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o Te Awa: Stories and Symbols
activities.description: In the classroom, students will work with resources from the Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website and local archives to deepen their research on the awa's stories. They will identify key symbols (e.g., mangō (shark), kōura (crayfish), water patterns) and learn their meanings, beginning to draft design ideas for their whakairo.

activities.title: Te Mahi Whakairo: The Art of Carving
activities.description: A local whakairo expert will lead a workshop on safe carving techniques using sustainable wood sources (e.g., reclaimed wood). Students will transfer their designs onto wood and begin the hands-on process of carving, focusing on the craftsmanship and intentionality behind each cut and symbol.

activities.title: Te Whakakotahitanga: Collaborative Installation
activities.description: In small groups, students will combine their individual carvings or create new pieces to form a collaborative art installation. This installation will represent a collective response to a modern issue facing the awa, such as creating a 'guardian figure' or a symbolic representation of water purity.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga: Exhibition and Reflection
activities.description: Students will curate a mini-exhibition of their work for the school and community. They will present their pieces, explaining the cultural narratives and personal connections, and engage in a poroporoaki (closing ceremony) to reflect on their role as kaitiaki of local stories and environments.

resources: Local kaumātua or knowledge holder from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Huia Smith)
Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website (www.kahungunu.iwi.nz) for pūrākau and historical information
Reclaimed wood from local sources (e.g., offcuts from a community woodwork shop)
Basic whakairo toolkits (mallets, chisels, gouges) and safety equipment

tasks: Completed individual whakairo piece with an artist statement explaining cultural symbols and narrative connections
Group presentation on the collaborative installation, addressing its message and connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Written reflection on the hīkoi experience and how it influenced their artistic choices

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the characteristics, uses, and meanings of objects and images from past and present cultures, particularly Māori arts like whakairo.
Developing Practical Knowledge: Use a range of media, methods, and technologies to create objects and images, exploring techniques and conventions of whakairo.
Communicating and Interpreting: Present and respond to objects and images, explaining ideas about their sources, uses, and cultural meanings.
Developing Ideas: Generate, develop, and refine visual ideas in response to a variety of motivations, including cultural and environmental stimuli.
